title: Psacalium brachycomum
taxonomy: Psacalium brachycomum is a species of flowering plant that belongs to the family Asteraceae. It is a member of the genus Psacalium, which is a group of plants commonly known as "tree daisies" or "milkweed trees." The genus Psacalium is further classified within the subfamily Mutisioideae. This subfamily is a part of the larger clade of plants known as the core eudicots. The classification of Psacalium brachycomum reflects its evolutionary relationships and shared characteristics with other plants in the Asteraceae family.

preparations: Psacalium brachycomum, also known as Coyote tobacco or Coyotillo, has been used in traditional medicine by indigenous communities in Mexico and other parts of Latin America. The plant is said to have various health benefits, including treating respiratory issues, skin conditions, and even snake bites. Herbal preparations of Psacalium brachycomum are typically made by infusing the leaves and stems in hot water or using the plant's roots in teas. intro: Psacalium brachycomum, also known as Mexican dandelion or Hierba de la reina, is a perennial herbaceous plant belonging to the Asteraceae family, native to Mexico and the southwestern United States. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health conditions, including digestive issues, skin problems, and respiratory infections, due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Psacalium brachycomum include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and triterpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, Psacalium brachycomum is often prepared as a decoction or infusion, and it is also used in the form of tinctures, capsules, and topical creams to alleviate symptoms of various health conditions.
